content:
A few months ago, I noticed something that completely changed how I looked at software development.
It wasn’t a production outage.
It wasn’t a failed deployment.
It wasn’t even a difficult technical problem.
It was a simple question that came up during a sprint planning session.
“Does anyone know where this API is being used?”
The room went quiet.
One developer thought only the mobile application consumed it.
Another believed an internal scheduler depended on it.
Someone else remembered adding a customer-specific implementation almost a year earlier.
Nobody was completely sure.
So instead of making the change, we started investigating.
We searched through multiple repositories.
Followed API calls across different services.
Read old pull requests.
Opened architecture diagrams.
Compared production logs.
Searched Slack conversations.
Nearly forty minutes later, we finally had our answer.
The API wasn’t complicated.
Finding its history was.
That afternoon, I wrote one sentence in my notebook.
We’re spending more time understanding our software than improving it.
At first, I assumed it was an isolated incident.
It wasn’t.
Once I started paying attention, I saw the same pattern everywhere.
A developer picked up a bug.
The first thirty minutes weren’t spent fixing it.
They were spent figuring out where to start.
Another developer wanted to introduce a new feature.
Before writing a single line of code, they needed to understand three existing services, two background jobs, and an old business rule that nobody had touched in months.
A code review turned into an architecture discussion because nobody could remember why a particular validation existed.
Every task began with the same activity.
Searching.
Not building.
Searching.
The more our platform grew, the more obvious the problem became.
We weren’t slowing down because writing software had become harder.
We were slowing down because understanding software had become harder.
Every Engineering Team Has This Person
If you’ve worked on a growing codebase, you’ve almost certainly met them.
The developer everyone messages before making an important change.
The one who immediately knows why production behaves differently from staging.
The one who remembers that a seemingly unused method is still called by an overnight scheduler.
The one who says,
“Don’t delete that. Something else still depends on it.”
They’re not always the most senior engineer.
They simply carry years of engineering decisions in their head.
Over time, they become the team’s unofficial search engine.
Questions that should have answers inside the system eventually reach one person.

• “Where is this endpoint used?”

• “Can I safely rename this field?”

• “Why are we validating this request twice?”

• “Who originally built this module?”

• “Has this production issue happened before?”
Those aren’t difficult questions.
They’re context questions.
The answers rarely exist in one place.
Part of the story lives inside the code.
Another part lives inside API documentation.
Database schemas explain something else.
Pull requests explain why a decision was made.
Production logs explain when it failed.
Architecture diagrams explain how services communicate.
Meeting notes explain the original business requirement.
Sometimes the final piece exists only because someone remembers a conversation from months ago.
Individually, each source is useful.
Together, they tell the complete story.
Unfortunately, they’re rarely connected.
That wasn’t a problem when our team was small.
With three developers, everyone roughly understood the entire system.
Need an answer?
Walk across the room.
Send a quick message.
Problem solved.
But software doesn’t stay small forever.
Projects multiply.
Repositories grow.
New developers join.
Services become independent.
The number of engineering decisions increases every week.
Eventually, nobody understands everything anymore.
Knowledge doesn’t disappear.
It becomes fragmented.
Without realizing it, we had built our biggest dependency.
Not a framework.
Not a database.
People.
Documentation Was Never the Problem
Like most engineering teams, our first instinct was simple.
We thought we needed better documentation.
So we documented everything.
API specifications.
Architecture diagrams.
Business rules.
Deployment guides.
Meeting summaries.
Release notes.
Feature documentation.
Internal wikis.
For a while, everything looked organized.
Then the software kept evolving.
Customers requested new features.
Edge cases appeared in production.
Business requirements changed.
Developers introduced improvements.
Old implementations disappeared.
The software moved forward.
The documentation slowly stayed behind.
Nobody ignored it intentionally.
Software simply changes faster than documents.
Eventually, something interesting happened.
Developers stopped treating documentation as the source of truth.
Instead, it became the starting point.
Every investigation looked almost identical.
Open the documentation.
Notice something doesn’t match production.
Open the repository.
Trace the request flow.
Jump between services.
Inspect database models.
Read commit history.
Open old pull requests.
Search production logs.
Ask whoever last modified the feature.
Only then did the actual work begin.
Documentation wasn’t failing.
Reality had simply become much larger than documentation could describe.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Cost Nobody Was Measuring
What surprised us most wasn’t the amount of time being lost.
It was where that time disappeared.
Nobody opened Jira and created a task called,
“Spend forty-five minutes remembering how checkout works.”
Nobody estimated,
“Investigate why this validation exists.”
Nobody planned for,
“Interrupt another developer because only they know the answer.”
Yet those activities happened every single day.
One interruption became another.
Developers switched between repositories.
Browser tabs multiplied.
Terminal windows stayed open for hours.
The deeper we looked, the more we realized we weren’t paying the cost once.
We were paying it repeatedly.
Every bug.
Every feature.
Every refactor.
Every onboarding session.
Every code review.
Every production incident.
The same questions surfaced again and again because the answers were difficult to find.
Not because they didn’t exist.
Because they were scattered.
The most experienced engineers spent a surprising amount of time answering questions they had already answered before.
New developers became dependent on them.
Instead of learning how the platform worked, they learned who to ask.
That wasn’t helping the team grow.
It was quietly slowing everyone down.
We Were Solving the Wrong Problem
Like many engineering teams, we experimented with different developer tools.
Some helped generate boilerplate.
Others explained unfamiliar syntax.
Some reviewed pull requests.
Others suggested improvements during development.
They were genuinely useful.
For isolated programming tasks, they saved time.
But something changed the moment we asked questions about our application.
The answers became generic.
Not because the tools were inaccurate.
Because they had no understanding of our engineering history.
They didn’t know why one endpoint behaved differently for enterprise customers.
They didn’t know which production incident introduced a particular validation.
They didn’t know why a seemingly unused function still existed.
They didn’t know which background service quietly depended on an internal event.
Those details weren’t hidden inside a programming language.
They were hidden inside years of engineering decisions.
That realization changed the direction of the project.
For weeks, we kept asking ourselves,
“How can we get better answers?”
Eventually, we realized we were asking the wrong question.
The better question was much simpler.
“How can every answer begin with the right context?”
That single question became the turning point.
Because once we stopped thinking about writing better answers…
We started thinking about organizing engineering knowledge.
And that changed everything.
We Started Looking in the Wrong Direction
Once we accepted that context was the real problem, our first instinct was to improve documentation even further.
Maybe we needed better diagrams.
Maybe we needed stricter documentation standards.
Maybe we needed more comments in the code.
So we tried.
Every new feature came with documentation.
Architecture diagrams became more detailed.
We encouraged developers to document their decisions.
For a while, it looked promising.
Then reality caught up again.
A production issue introduced an exception.
A customer requested custom business logic.
A deadline forced us to take a shortcut.
A feature evolved in a direction nobody expected.
The code changed.
The documentation didn’t.
Within a few weeks, we were back where we started.
That was when we realized something important.
Documentation doesn’t become outdated because engineers don’t care.
It becomes outdated because software changes much faster than people can document it.
The more active a project becomes, the harder it is for documentation to represent reality.
At that point, we stopped asking,
“How do we write better documentation?”
Instead, we asked,
“What already stays up to date automatically?”
The answer was obvious.
The code.
The Code Already Knew Everything
When you think about it, almost every engineering decision eventually ends up somewhere inside the codebase.
Business rules become validation.
Requirements become services.
Database changes become migrations.
Integrations become APIs.
Production fixes become commits.
The source code wasn’t missing information.
It was hiding it.
The challenge wasn’t creating knowledge.
It was extracting it.
That completely changed our approach.
Instead of asking developers to maintain another knowledge base, we decided to build one directly from the engineering workflow they were already following.
No additional effort.
No extra documentation.
No manual uploads.
If the engineering team was already writing code, reviewing pull requests and deploying features, the platform should quietly learn from those activities.
Not interrupt them.
Looking Beyond Source Code
Very quickly we discovered another problem.
The code explains how something works.
It doesn’t always explain why it exists.
Imagine opening a service and seeing this.
if (customer.isEnterprise) {
return bypassValidation();
}Technically, the code is correct.
But why does enterprise bypass validation?
Was it a customer request?
A temporary workaround?
A production hotfix?
A contractual requirement?
The answer usually wasn’t inside the file.
It lived somewhere else.
Sometimes inside a pull request.
Sometimes inside a Jira ticket.
Sometimes inside a production incident report.
Sometimes inside an architecture discussion.
Sometimes only inside the memory of the engineer who wrote it.
That’s when we realized something else.
Engineering knowledge isn’t one thing.
It’s an ecosystem.
Mapping Our Engineering Knowledge
Before building anything, we spent several days doing something surprisingly simple.
We wrote down every place where engineering knowledge existed.
The list became much longer than we expected.
It wasn’t just repositories.
It included almost every tool the engineering team used.

• Source code

• API specifications

• Database schemas

• DTOs

• Validation rules

• Pull requests

• Commit history

• Internal documentation

• Architecture diagrams

• Production logs

• Monitoring dashboards

• Bug reports

• Deployment history

• Configuration files

• Feature flags

• Release notes
Every one of those sources answered different questions.
Repositories explained implementation.
Database schemas explained relationships.
Logs explained failures.
Pull requests explained decisions.
Monitoring explained behavior.
Documentation explained business requirements.
No single source told the complete story.
Together, they did.
